chain_of_thought: Top native frame with logic is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum(param_1). It parses ASN.1 element children by walking a length iVar3 = Compute_ASN1_ValueLength(param_1) and indexing into *(param_1+0x10). No allocators/sanitizers here, so this is the app-level parsing logic, but there is no obvious memory write, only reads and arithmetic on indices, with early returns on invalid tag classes or value length -1.

Crash reason string is just the function name, suggesting fuzzing detected a failure in this parser (likely returning 0 or misbehaving), but provided decompilation shows no out-of-bounds access given realistic invariants: the for-loop condition uses local_28 != iVar3; and inside the loop, local_28 is only advanced via local_28 = local_28 + local_30 + local_34 with local_30 and local_34 derived from other ASN.1 helper functions for this same buffer. The body returns 0 when value length is -1 or tag invalid; there is no unchecked memcpy or pointer arithmetic visible here.

The JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID takes a Java byte[] (param_3) via the JNI env function pointer table (*param_1 + 0x5c0 / 0x558). It obtains its length iVar2, mallocs __size bytes, memset and memcpy that buffer, then passes it into CERT_Load and CERT_GetCertPolicy_PolicyID. Those in turn eventually call Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. The length for malloc and memcpy is exactly the Java array length and is used consistently; there is no integer overflow or off-by-one on the JNI side.

The crash is attributed to Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, but based on the code we see, any invalid ASN.1 state leads to returning 0; there is no dereference of null param_1 (guarded: if param_1 == 0 then simply return 0). Buffer accesses are to *(long *)(param_1+0x10) + local_28 where local_28 is bounded by iVar3, which presumably is the same total length of the ASN.1 buffer (Compute_ASN1_ValueLength). Unless Compute_ASN1_ValueLength returns an incorrect length, there is no memory violation here, and we have not been shown its implementation.

Given the fuzz harness, fuzz_one_input just feeds arbitrary data as a certificate to CERT_Load. In realistic app usage, the certificate buffer will come from the banking app’s logic (server-provided certificate or file), not from an attacker-controlled parameter; and even if malformed, CERT_Load chain is performing structural checks (returns 0 on invalid tags/lengths). The crash’s label could correspond to internal ASSERT/abort or unhandled return, but we do not see any abort() or sanitizer; it more likely is the fuzzer using the function name as "termination reason" after some non-exploitable failure.

JNI code around malloc/memcpy also lacks basic error checks (e.g., __s == NULL before memset/memcpy), but that is only exploitable if malloc(large) fails, which on Android typically results in process abort anyway and is not attacker-meaningfully exploitable for this local JNI method. Moreover, memset/memcpy use the same validated size from JNI, so there is no overflow of the heap allocation.

Therefore, with currently visible code, there is no demonstrated memory safety violation or attacker-controllable unsafe behavior. The crash signature is most likely a harness-induced or benign logical failure in ASN.1 parsing rather than a security vulnerability.
reasons: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum only reads within bounds derived from Compute_ASN1_ValueLength and returns 0 on invalid structures; no obvious memory write or overflow.
JNI method jGetPolicyOID correctly allocates a buffer of the Java array length and copies using the same length, without integer overflow or out-of-bounds.

Crash reason appears to be a parser failure under fuzzed ASN.1 input, not a sanitizer-detected memory corruption, and typical app usage does not expose this as attacker-controlled beyond malformed cert causing DoS at most.

evidence.note: Loops within overall ASN.1 length; returns 0 on invalid value length; no writes into this buffer.
Size for malloc/memcpy is exactly the Java array length; no overflow.
Certificate parsing and policy extraction use fixed-size stack buffer (0x400) for output, potentially limiting overflow; implementation details of CERT_* not shown.

recommendations: Review and test Compute_ASN1_ValueLength and related ASN.1 helpers under fuzzing to ensure they never return inconsistent lengths that could break invariants.
Add robust error handling in CERT_Load and related functions to gracefully reject malformed certificates instead of propagating unexpected states.
Optionally add NULL-checks around malloc results in JNI methods to avoid UB if allocation fails under extreme memory pressure.
assumptions: Compute_ASN1_ValueLength returns a correct non-negative total length of the ASN.1 buffer and not a value exceeding the backing allocation.
CERT_Load and related ASN.1 helper functions do not perform unchecked writes beyond allocated buffers.
Fuzz harness termination label "Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum" corresponds to a logical failure, not a sanitizer-detected memory violation.
limitations: Implementation of Compute_ASN1_ValueLength and Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO was not inspected; if they miscompute, they might lead to OOB reads.
CERT_Load, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT bodies were not analyzed; hidden memory-safety bugs there cannot be ruled out.
Crash context (signal type, registers, exact faulting address) was not provided, limiting precise root-cause determination.

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame below allocator/sanitizer: crash in __memcpy, next frame is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, which is application-level parsing code for DER/ASN. This is our starting point.

Even though tools are required, we have only symbolic stack and library map, no direct addresses. However, we can infer from names that the code is a custom DER/ASN.1 decoder converting DER-encoded certificate data into an internal X509 structure, then loading certificate and extracting Policy OID via jGetPolicyOID.

A memcpy-based crash in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write due to incorrect length/offset handling when parsing attacker-controlled certificate bytes. Typical issues: trusting length fields from DER without validating against total buffer size; miscomputing child element boundaries in Get_DER_Child; copying to fixed-size internal buffers without bounds checks.

Backward reasoning along stack: __memcpy <- Decode_DER_ASN1_ST <- Get_DER_Child <- Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT <- CERT_Load <- Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID. CERT_Load likely receives a DER/ASN1 blob (certificate or policy extension) from JNI. The fuzz harness fakes this input, but in real app, certificates or data blobs are plausibly derived from external sources (smart card, server, QR, etc.). Thus, DER bytes can be attacker-controlled or at least untrusted.

Since the crash is in memcpy, not in harness-specific code, and is reached through a realistic parsing chain (CERT_Load / ASN1 decode), this looks like genuine unsafe parsing logic. No evidence of bounds checking is given; memcpy itself does not enforce limits. The crash (Process Termination: __memcpy) indicates runtime detected overlapping/invalid regions or segfault due to invalid dest/src pointer or size, consistent with buffer overflow or out-of-bounds read.

There is no evidence this is harness-only misuse like passing null where production never would. The fuzz harness calls the same JNI method jGetPolicyOID used by Java. Unless Java always constrains input to well-formed small certificates (unlikely), malformed or crafted input could trigger the same path. Thus, we classify as likely memory-corruption vulnerability with moderate-high confidence.

CWE classification: primarily CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write) or CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read). Since memcpy can corrupt memory if dest size is smaller than copied length, and crash occurs in memcpy rather than a graceful length check, CWE-787 is appropriate. Impact: likely at least high (memory corruption in native code from untrusted input), potentially RCE under right conditions.

Limitations: No direct decompilation of functions (decode, Get_DER_Child, CERT_Load) or Java call graph. No concrete code snippet. Cannot see validation logic (if any) around lengths; assessment infers usual vulnerabilities from pattern of crash and function names. Severity assumes attacker can influence certificate/DER input (remote or local).
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from custom DER/ASN.1 decoder (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST), indicating unsafe memory copy during parsing.
Call chain (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Get_DER_Child -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> jGetPolicyOID) matches realistic certificate/ASN1 parsing, not a harness-only stub.
DER/ASN.1 length and structure fields are typically derived from untrusted input; lack of explicit bounds checking before memcpy suggests potential out-of-bounds write/read.
Fuzzing-induced crash demonstrates malformed input can reach and break native parsing routines, implying a reachable DoS and likely memory corruption under realistic misuse.

evidence.note: Top application-level frame above __memcpy in stack trace; likely performs memcpy based on DER/ASN.1 length fields without sufficient bounds checking.
Intermediate parser function walking DER structure; miscalculated child size/offset can propagate incorrect length to memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
Converts ASN.1 data to X509_CERT; suggests that DER blob and its extensions are treated as untrusted input and processed natively.
Certificate loading routine receiving DER/ASN.1 bytes from JNI or higher layers, implying attacker influence over parsed data.

recommendations: Decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and CERT_Load to identify all memcpy/memmove calls and add explicit bounds checks against the actual buffer size before copying.
Introduce centralized DER/ASN.1 validation that ensures all length and offset fields are within the input buffer before any memory access, and return errors instead of continuing on malformed data.
Add robust negative tests/fuzzing for certificate/DER parsing (including large, nested, and malformed elements) and enable ASan/UBSan builds to catch similar memory issues during development.
assumptions: DER/ASN.1 input processed by CERT_Load ultimately originates from data that an attacker can influence (e.g., external certificates or tokens).
No comprehensive bounds checking is implemented around memcpy inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or its callers.
The fuzz harness exercises the same JNI entry path jGetPolicyOID as the real app would, without adding unrealistic arguments beyond malformed DER bytes.
limitations: No direct access to decompiled native code or exact memcpy call sites; analysis is based on stack trace and symbol names only.
Cannot inspect the Java side (UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID) to confirm how inputs are sourced and whether any pre-validation exists.
Exact nature of the memcpy misuse (out-of-bounds write vs read vs invalid pointer) is unknown, limiting precision of CWE and exploitability assessment.
